hook_full.text: No road out. So Quinhagak learned to fly its own drones.

pull_quote.text: We get weekly calls for search and rescue. Sometimes daily.

body.text: In a roadless stretch of the Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta, a lost boat or an overdue traveler is a life-and-death search. Quinhagak's drone team answers those calls from the air, week after week.

headline.text: The sky is the only road.

body.text: No roads reach Quinhagak or its neighbors on the Kuskokwim coast. Rivers and airspace are the only ways through. A local pilot, instructor Sean Gleason says, knows the traffic patterns better than anyone from outside.

On June 9, a dozen trainees from across rural Alaska came to Quinhagak to learn to fly. The local course runs 14 weeks toward an FAA Part 107 license on an Alaska DOT grant, one seat each for Quinhagak, Eek, Goodnews, and Platinum.

lines: WANTS TO COUNT REINDEER BY THERMAL DRONE

body.text: The drones first came to map Nunalleq, an ancestral Yup'ik village eroding into the Bering Sea. The pilots stayed, and turned the same eyes on the living delta.

Nalaquq, the village-owned company, wrote its own machine vision. A neural network spots a small aluminum boat on a search grid. A polarized-lens rig counts salmon in the river. The name means it has been found.

headline.text: Most of the eyes are human.

body.text: Be exact about the machine. A neural network finds boats. A lens rig counts salmon. But people fly every drone, and the thermal reindeer counts and the archaeology are human eyes reading pictures, not algorithms.

headline.text: Own the eye, don't rent it.

body.text: Elsewhere the sensor, the model, and the data belong to outside companies. In Quinhagak the pilots, the drones, and the machine vision belong to the village that flies over its own land, water, and people.

ask.text: Who should own the machine that watches your community?

document_title: No Road Out. Quinhagak Flies Its Own Eyes
first_comment:
Sources for this one, primary first.
KYUK, Quinhagak locals bridge drones and traditional knowledge, now they are teaching others, July 14 2026, https://www.kyuk.org/science-and-environment/2026-07-14/quinhagak-locals-bridge-drones-and-traditional-knowledge-now-theyre-teaching-others-how-to-do-it
Alaska Public Media, Alaska News Nightly, western Alaska drone program takes flight, July 14 2026, https://alaskapublic.org/programs/alaska-news-nightly/2026-07-14/alaska-news-nightly-tuesday-july-14-2026
Nalaquq, the Alaska Native-owned subsidiary of Qanirtuuq, https://nalaquq.com/about-us/
JCMC, the peer-reviewed paper behind the search-and-rescue vessel-detection CNN and the salmon-counting pipeline, https://academic.oup.com/jcmc/article/28/5/zmad030/7248802
Every Alaska AI decision we track, with live deadlines, lives at alaskaaihq.com
If you know Quinhagak or Nalaquq firsthand, reply and add what the reporting missed.
